topic: Attributes - Lint Control: allow, warn, deny, forbid

turns.content: Hey, I've been reading about Rust's lint attributes like `allow`, `warn`, `deny`, and `forbid`. But I'm still a bit confused. Could you explain the hierarchy and how to scope them properly?

Sure! The hierarchy is straightforward: `allow` is the least restrictive, then `warn`, followed by `deny`, and finally `forbid`. When combined with attributes like `#[allow(warnings)]` or `#![deny(unsafe_code)]`, these control how lint rules are enforced. For scoping, you can use `#[allow(...)]` on items (like functions or structs), `#[warn(...)]` at module level, and `#![forbid(...)]` in the crate root.
Okay, that makes sense. So if I put a `#![deny(warnings)]` at the crate root, it will catch all warnings, right?

Yes, exactly! That's why putting something like `#![deny(warnings)]` in your CI pipeline is common — to enforce no warnings on build. But be aware: some lints are less strict than 'warnings,' so sometimes you might need to manually adjust the hierarchy or add `allow` exceptions.
Hmm, wait, I thought `#![deny(warnings)]` would catch all warnings but maybe miss some? How do I make sure everything is caught?

Good question. If you're worried about missing lints, you might want to combine with `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]`, which is very strict and catches unsafe usage. Alternatively, if your crate is purely safe, then yes, `#![deny(warnings)]` will catch almost everything. But always double-check: some lints are categorized differently or have nuanced rules.
Okay, so let's say I want to enforce all warnings but also explicitly deny a few specific unsafe patterns. Should I use `#![deny(warnings)]` plus exceptions for unsafe code?

Yes! For example: `#![deny(warnings)]`, then add `#[allow(unsafe_code)]` inside modules where unsafe is acceptable, or even better: define a custom lint with clippy (if you're using it). This way, you can have broad linting while fine-tuning for specific cases.
Right. And what about clippy lints? How do they fit into this hierarchy?

Clippy is a separate lint tool that extends the Rust compiler's built-in lints with more checks. You can configure both together: `#![warn(clippy::all)]` will warn on all clippy rules, and you can override this at finer levels if needed. For example, if you want to suppress a specific clippy lint inside a module, use `#[allow(clippy::specific_lint)]`. This helps maintain balance between strictness and developer convenience.
Got it! So the hierarchy is `allow < warn < deny < forbid` with scoping at crate root, modules, or items. And clippy lints can be combined like normal lint rules?
Exactly! For example: `#![deny(warnings)]`, `#[warn(clippy::all)]`, and then fine-tune with exceptions as needed.
Hmm, wait. I thought maybe clippy lints are separate from built-in lints? So how do you scope them?

Ah, good catch! Clippy lints also have a hierarchy: `allow`, `warn`, `deny`. You can use `#![deny(clippy::all)]` to disable all clippy warnings, or specify individual lints. For example: `#[warn(clippy::pedantic)]` is very strict and catches even stylistic issues.
Okay, so I should be careful about adding too many deny rules to avoid breaking build pipelines?

Absolutely! Start with `#![deny(warnings)]` in CI and gradually add specific denies. This way, you can catch warnings without being overwhelmed by errors. Also, remember that sometimes lint rules evolve or change, so it's good practice to regularly review your deny list.
Thanks for the detailed explanation! I think now I understand how to structure my lint attributes effectively.
You're welcome! That's great. If you have any more questions about lints, scoping, or clippy, feel free to ask.

topic: Attributes - Lint Control: allow, warn, deny, forbid

turns.content: Hey, I'm debugging a build error in production and it seems like some warnings are being treated as errors. How do attributes like #[allow], #[warn], #[deny], and #[forbid] work and what's the best practice?

Great question! In Rust, those `#[allow]`, `#[warn]`, `#[deny]`, and `#[forbid]` attributes control lint behavior. The hierarchy is: forbid < deny < warn < allow. This means that if you have a crate root with `#![deny(warnings)]`, it will override all less restrictive settings further down the module tree.
So, if I put `#![deny(warnings)]` at the crate root, then every file in that crate has to be warning-free? What if one of them still shows a warning?

Exactly! That's why you want to be careful. If any module or item violates `deny(warnings)`, the build will fail. This is great for enforcing strict quality, but sometimes it leads to false positives, especially if a crate has dependencies that don't follow your rules.
Oh right — I thought maybe there's a way to override at a module or item level, like `#[allow(warn)]` or `#[deny(unsafe_code)]`. But you said the hierarchy is forbid < deny < warn < allow?

topic: Attributes - Lint Control: allow, warn, deny, forbid

turns.content: Hey! I'm back in the game after five years. I'm trying to set up a strict linting policy for my new project using clippy and I want to make sure unsafe code is never allowed, but I noticed something weird when I tried #![deny(warnings)] at the crate root level.

Welcome back! That attribute hierarchy can be tricky. The issue with placing #![deny(warnings)] at the module or item level is that it won't apply to functions in other modules unless you use #[allow(...)] explicitly there. For a crate-wide policy, especially one needing to block unsafe code entirely, you should probably target #![deny(unsafe_code)] instead.
Ah okay, so if I put that at the top of main.rs it stops clippy from complaining about unsafe blocks? But what if I have a helper function in another module that needs to be safe though?

You can override specific lints for individual items by using #[allow(unsafe_code)] or #[allow(clippy::...]. The attribute hierarchy is allow < warn < deny < forbid, so placing #![deny(warnings)] on a module will only affect that module and its children, not the whole crate.
Got it. So I should probably keep #![deny(unsafe_code)] at the root but maybe add #[allow(...)] where needed? What about warnings though?

Exactly. For CI pipelines, you often want to warn on style issues while denying unsafe blocks entirely. You can use #![warn(unused_variables)] or similar at module level if needed without blocking the whole crate.
That makes sense. One more thing though, what happens when I run clippy with --all-targets and it says warnings are ignored?

The attribute hierarchy determines which lints get suppressed or enforced globally versus locally. If you see warnings being ignored despite #![deny(...], check if the deny directive is scoped correctly and not overridden by an allow on that specific item.

Oh! I think I figured it out. The #![deny(warnings)] was only applying to my current module, but clippy ran across all targets so the warnings were being ignored elsewhere unless I explicitly allowed them per item.
That's exactly right! You now understand how scoping works with attributes and why #![deny(unsafe_code)] at crate root is ideal for safety-critical codebases. Nice aha-moment!
